content:
37 min read
Just now

--

Part 1 built the blueprint — the demand inventory, legacy table triage, entity mapping, and the layer-stamped source-to-target map dividing all logic between the database and Power BI. Part 2 built the database end — layers, dimensions, facts, loads, and the reconciliation harness. Part 3 builds everything stamped L4 and L5 — the semantic model — and then runs the migration itself: parallel operation, validation, user acceptance, and cutover.

If the previous two parts did their jobs, this part begins from an unusual position of strength: the semantic model gets to be thin. The gold layer delivers tested, business-named, declared-grain star schemas; the STM says exactly which logic belongs up here (user-context calculations, presentation, security) and which was already handled below. What remains is the craft that only the semantic layer can perform — and the discipline of not performing anything else.
That framing matters because the semantic model is where migrations historically bloat. Power BI makes it easy to fix data problems in Power Query, encode business rules in calculated columns, and patch metric inconsistencies visual by visual — and every one of those conveniences is a small withdrawal against the contract Part 1 signed. This article is therefore two things at once: a complete construction manual for the semantic layer (topology, storage, relationships, measures, security, performance), and a set of guardrails keeping it the thin, fast, trustworthy layer the architecture promised. It closes with the part no modeling series should skip: how the whole migration actually lands — parallel running, three-level validation, user acceptance, cutover, and decommission.
Section 1: Topology — How Many Models, Owned by Whom
The first semantic decision is structural and organizational at once: how many semantic models exist, and how do reports relate to them?
The default: one shared semantic model per bus matrix (or per major domain), thin reports connected live. All 20 in-scope reports from the worked example connect to a small number of published models — often exactly one, since the bus matrix was designed as one integrated star field. No report embeds its own copy of the model. The reasons compound:

• One truth per metric. A measure exists once, owned once, fixed once. The legacy estate’s disease — five subtly different [Revenue] definitions across five PBIX files — is structurally impossible when reports are thin.

• Parallel work at scale. Ten report builders rebuild pages simultaneously against one stable model contract while one or two modelers own measures. This is the semantic-layer mirror of Part 2’s slice parallelism, and it is what makes the report-rebuild phase fast.

• Independent lifecycle. The model deploys, versions, and tests as its own artifact — source-controlled in PBIP format, promoted through dev/test/prod like any other code, with reports promoting separately.
When to split into multiple models: genuinely disjoint audiences with disjoint dimensions (the HR star and the finance star share little beyond Date), hard security isolation requirements, or capacity/size boundaries. When not to split: team convenience, report folder structure, or “this dashboard feels special.” Splitting along anything other than the bus matrix’s natural seams recreates the drift problem with extra steps. If two models must share a dimension conceptually, they each import it from the same gold table — conformance survives the split because gold owns it.
Composite-model caution for migrations specifically: Power BI allows models built on top of other models (DirectQuery to a published semantic model, extended locally). It is a legitimate advanced tool and a terrible migration default — it layers refresh dependencies, complicates lineage exactly when you’re trying to prove lineage, and reintroduces “who owns this measure” ambiguity. During migration: one layer of semantic models over gold, nothing stacked. Revisit composability as a phase-two governance topic if self-service demands it.
Ownership completes topology: each model has a named owner (typically the modeler/architect from Part 1) who is the sole merger of measure changes; report builders request measures through the same lightweight flow that Part 2 used for gold changes — an STM/dictionary row first, then implementation. The measure dictionary from the reverse engineering, updated to as-built, doubles as the request catalogue: most “new measure” requests during rebuild turn out to be existing measures under legacy names.
Section 2: Storage Modes and Refresh — The Physics Decisions
Storage mode decisions look technical but they set the user experience and the refresh economics for years. The playbook keeps them boring:
Default: Import everything. In-memory columnar storage (VertiPaq) gives interactive visuals their speed, tolerates source downtime, and unlocks the full DAX surface. Over a well-built gold layer, import sizes are dramatically smaller than beginners fear — Part 2’s design already removed the classic bloat (debris columns died in triage; datetimes split; text lives on small dimensions; facts carry keys and skinny numerics).
Deviate only on evidence, per table, in writing:

• Too big to import (after cleanup, measured against capacity memory, projected via a representative load and VertiPaq Analyzer extrapolation): first response is incremental refresh (partition the fact by date; refresh only recent partitions — over gold’s incremental loads this aligns naturally, and the RangeStart/RangeEnd parameters simply mirror the pipeline’s watermark). Second response: aggregations — a small imported summary table (built in gold, per Part 2’s rule that agg tables are derivable, never authoritative) answering high-level visuals automatically, with DirectQuery on the detail fact and Dual mode on the shared dimensions so they can serve both paths. That composite is the standard big-data pattern; adopt it as a measured response, never as insurance.

• Genuinely fresh-data requirements (business-verified minutes-latency, not “would be nice”): DirectQuery for the specific tables involved, with expectations set explicitly — every click becomes a source query, so this choice trades interactivity for freshness and should cover as little of the model as possible.
Refresh orchestration belongs to the pipeline, not the calendar: the semantic refresh triggers on gold-load completion (API-triggered from the orchestrator), so users never see a half-loaded day and the reconciliation harness compares like with like. During parallel running, pin the legacy refresh schedule into the comparison logic too — half the “discrepancies” of early parallel operation are simply the two stacks refreshing at different times, and encoding both watermarks into the Tier-2 harness eliminates that noise class at the source.
Section 3: Connecting to Gold — The Deliberately Boring Power Query
Per the contract, the semantic model’s Power Query is a connection layer. The entire specification: connect through parameters (server/database/schema as parameters so environments switch without edits, mirroring gold’s deployment stages); select the gold tables for this model; select only needed columns (gold may carry audit columns the model doesn’t import); confirm every step folds (view native query — a non-folding step over a big table is a refresh bottleneck announcing itself); done. Renames should be rare because gold already speaks business language — the exceptions are presentation-only tweaks the STM records.
The hygiene items that cost minutes now and weeks later if skipped: turn off Auto date/time globally (it silently attaches a hidden calendar to every date column, bloating the model and bypassing your real date dimension); set data types deliberately at the source of truth (they should simply flow from gold’s types — a type “fix” needed here is a gold bug to report, not a step to add); disable load on any parameter-driven helper queries; and no transformation logic, per the placement matrix, with displaced work filed back to the gold backlog rather than absorbed. The two sanctioned exceptions carry paperwork: tiny typed-in presentation tables (a sort-order helper, a field-parameter scaffold) and explicitly ticketed, time-boxed migration shims.
A note for teams on capacities that support it: newer storage/connection modes that read lakehouse tables directly can replace the import pipeline entirely. The playbook is deliberately agnostic — those modes change how bytes move, not the modeling: the same gold contract, the same thin connection layer, the same everything-downstream. Choose per your platform’s current guidance; the design survives the choice.
Section 4: Relationships — Five Defaults and the Reasoning Under Them
Wire each star as designed: dimension key → fact key. Then apply five defaults with the stubbornness reserved for naming conventions, because relationship exotica is the number-one source of “why is this number weird” tickets in inherited models — your reverse engineering likely proved this firsthand.
Default 1 — One-to-many, single direction, active. Dimensions filter facts; facts do not filter dimensions; ~95% of relationships look exactly like this. Predictability is the product: a filter’s path is legible from the diagram, so every number’s context is explainable.
Default 2 — Bidirectional filtering is a scalpel, not a setting. “Both” direction makes slicers cross-filter beautifully in the demo and then quietly introduces ambiguous filter paths, performance costs on large models, and numbers that change because an unrelated visual changed. The narrow legitimate uses: deliberately filtered bridge traversal (below), and occasional dimension-reduction slicers implemented instead with measure-level CROSSFILTER so the behavior is visible in code rather than ambient in the model. If a requirement seems to need bidirectional everywhere, the model shape is wrong — usually a missing bridge or a mis-assigned grain.
Default 3 — Many-to-many via bridges, not the M:N relationship type. Gold shipped bridge tables for the genuine M:N realities (Part 2, Section 2.4). Wire them: dimension → bridge (one-to-many), bridge → fact or bridge → dimension with the filter direction chosen deliberately — and the counting semantics (overlapping vs. weighted) implemented in measures exactly as the business signed. The native M:N relationship type removes the guardrails that make mistakes visible; treat it as expert equipment, not a shortcut.
Default 4 — Role-playing dates: one active relationship, USERELATIONSHIP measures for the rest — until slicing demand says otherwise. The fact carries invoice_date_key and ship_date_key; relate invoice date active (matching the dominant metric usage from the demand inventory), and implement shipping-view metrics as CALCULATE([X], USERELATIONSHIP(fct[ship_date_key], dim_date[date_key])). Escalate to a second physical date table (a gold view dim_ship_date) only when users need to slice everything by the second role — the cost is a second calendar users must understand, so it's a UX decision, made per model, documented either way. Mark the primary date table as the model's official date table.
Default 5 — No relationships between fact tables, ever. Cross-process analysis flows through conformed dimensions — the entire reason Part 1 planned them and Part 2 enforced them. Actuals vs. budget at different grains works through the shared dimension’s hierarchy (the budget fact keys to the rollup level, per Part 2’s shrunken-dimension pattern); the user’s matrix slices both facts by the same Product hierarchy and the numbers land side by side with no fact-to-fact join anywhere. The urge to relate facts directly is always a symptom: either a missing conformed dimension or an unagreed grain — return to the bus matrix.
Wire, then verify mechanically: every relationship in the model should be predicted
